Topics.Title: Introduction to Binary Trees
Topics.Summary: Defines binary trees as a foundational data structure and explains core terminology including nodes, edges, roots, leaves, and parent-child relationships. Establishes the structural rules that distinguish binary trees from other tree types.

Elements.Title: What Is a Binary Tree?
Elements.BodyText: A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure composed of nodes connected by edges, where each node holds a value and links to at most two child nodes.
Elements.Notes: Binary trees are foundational in computer science and underpin many advanced structures such as heaps, binary search trees, and expression trees.

Items.Text: Unlike linear structures such as arrays or linked lists, binary trees organize data in a branching, parent-child hierarchy.
The 'binary' constraint means no node can have more than two children, distinguishing it from general trees where any number of children is allowed.
This bounded branching factor gives binary trees predictable structural properties useful for efficient searching and sorting.

Elements.Title: Nodes and Edges
Elements.BodyText: Nodes are the fundamental units of a binary tree, each storing a data value and references to its children, while edges are the directional links connecting a parent node to a child node.
Elements.Notes: In a JavaScript implementation, a node is typically represented as an object with properties for the value, a left pointer, and a right pointer.

Items.Text: Every node contains a payload (the stored data) and up to two pointers: one to a left child and one to a right child.
An edge represents a single parent-to-child relationship; in a tree with N nodes there are always exactly N−1 edges.
Edges are directional, flowing from parent down to child, which enforces the hierarchical structure of the tree.

Elements.Title: The Root Node
Elements.BodyText: The root is the single topmost node of a binary tree and serves as the unique entry point through which all other nodes are reachable.
Elements.Notes: A valid binary tree has exactly one root. If a tree is empty, it has no root and is considered a null or empty tree.

Items.Text: The root has no parent; it is the only node in the tree with an in-degree of zero.
Every other node in the tree can be reached by following edges downward from the root.
The root sits at depth 0 (or level 1, depending on convention), and all measurements of tree height begin from it.

Elements.Title: Leaf Nodes
Elements.BodyText: A leaf node is any node that has no children — both its left and right child references are null — representing the endpoints of the tree.
Elements.Notes: Leaf nodes are significant in traversal algorithms because they form the base cases for recursive operations on the tree.

Items.Text: Leaves reside at the bottom of the tree and have an out-degree of zero, meaning they point to no further nodes.
A tree containing only a root node with no children means the root itself is also a leaf.
The number and distribution of leaf nodes affect tree balance and the efficiency of tree operations.

Elements.Title: Parent-Child Relationships
Elements.BodyText: In a binary tree, every node except the root has exactly one parent, and each node can be the parent of zero, one, or two children, forming a strict hierarchical relationship.
Elements.Notes: These relationships define the ancestry path from any node back to the root, which is important for operations like deletion that require locating a node's parent.

Items.Text: A parent node directly connects to its children via left and right edges; a child node stores no explicit reference back to its parent unless the implementation adds one.
Sibling nodes share the same parent; in a binary tree, a node can have at most one sibling.
The parent-child relationship is the building block of all structural concepts in a tree, including subtrees, depth, and height.

Elements.Title: The Binary Constraint: Structural Rules
Elements.BodyText: The defining rule of a binary tree is that every node may have at most two children, conventionally referred to as the left child and the right child.
Elements.Notes: This rule is what separates binary trees from general trees (unlimited children) and from other specialized structures like ternary trees (up to three children).

Items.Text: The left and right designation is not merely a label — it carries positional meaning that algorithms rely on, especially in binary search trees.
A node with one child is valid; the single child must be explicitly identified as either the left or the right child, not just 'a child'.
Enforcing a maximum of two children per node bounds the tree's branching factor and enables the efficient O(log n) performance characteristics associated with balanced binary trees.

Topics.Title: Node Structure and Tree Anatomy
Topics.Summary: Examines how individual nodes are constructed, including data storage and left and right child pointers. Covers key tree properties such as height, depth, and balance.

Elements.Title: Anatomy of a Binary Tree Node
Elements.BodyText: A binary tree node is the fundamental building block of the tree structure, containing three core components: a data field and two child references.
Elements.Notes: In JavaScript, a node is typically implemented as a class or object: { value: data, left: null, right: null }.

Items.Text: Each node stores a data value, which can be a number, string, or any other data type.
The left pointer references the node's left child subtree, or null if no left child exists.
The right pointer references the node's right child subtree, or null if no right child exists.
A node with no children (both pointers null) is called a leaf node.

Elements.Title: Root, Parent, and Child Relationships
Elements.BodyText: Binary trees are organized through hierarchical relationships between nodes, starting from a single entry point called the root.
Elements.Notes: Visualizing the tree as a family tree analogy helps: the root is the ancestor of all nodes, and every non-root node has exactly one parent.

Items.Text: The root is the topmost node in the tree and has no parent; it is the single entry point to the entire structure.
A parent node is any node that has one or two child nodes connected below it.
Each node can have at most two children, distinguishing binary trees from general trees.
Nodes sharing the same parent are called sibling nodes.

Elements.Title: Node Depth
Elements.BodyText: Depth measures how far a specific node is from the root of the tree, providing a way to locate nodes within the hierarchy.
Elements.Notes: For example, the root has a depth of 0, its direct children have a depth of 1, and grandchildren have a depth of 2.

Items.Text: Depth is defined as the number of edges on the path from the root down to a given node.
The root node always has a depth of 0.
Depth increases by one for each level you move away from the root toward the leaves.

Elements.Title: Tree Height
Elements.BodyText: Height is a property that describes the overall size of a tree or subtree, measured as the longest path from a node down to a leaf.
Elements.Notes: A tree with only a root node has a height of 0. An empty tree is often defined as having a height of -1.

Items.Text: The height of a node is the number of edges on the longest downward path from that node to a leaf.
The height of the entire tree is the height of its root node.
Height is critical for analyzing the efficiency of tree operations, as many algorithms run in O(h) time where h is the height.

Elements.Title: Subtrees and Tree Levels
Elements.BodyText: A binary tree can be understood recursively as a root node connected to a left subtree and a right subtree, each of which is itself a binary tree.
Elements.Notes: This recursive definition is foundational to implementing traversal algorithms and recursive operations on trees.

Items.Text: A subtree consists of a node and all of its descendants, forming a valid binary tree on its own.
Every node in a tree is the root of its own subtree.
A tree level groups all nodes at the same depth, with the root alone occupying level 0.
The number of nodes at each level can at most double compared to the level above it.

Elements.Title: Tree Balance
Elements.BodyText: Balance refers to how evenly the nodes of a tree are distributed between left and right subtrees, directly impacting the performance of tree operations.
Elements.Notes: An extremely unbalanced tree (where all nodes chain to one side) degrades to the performance of a linked list, with O(n) search time instead of O(log n).

Items.Text: A balanced binary tree is one where the heights of the left and right subtrees of every node differ by at most one.
Balance ensures that the tree height remains close to log₂(n), where n is the number of nodes.
An unbalanced tree can have significantly greater height, degrading the efficiency of insertion, deletion, and search operations.
Maintaining balance is the goal of self-balancing tree structures such as AVL trees and Red-Black trees.

Topics.Title: Binary Search Trees (BST) Concepts
Topics.Summary: Introduces the binary search tree as a specialized binary tree where left child values are less than the parent and right child values are greater. Explains how this ordering property enables efficient search operations.

Elements.Title: The BST Ordering Property
Elements.BodyText: A Binary Search Tree is a specialized binary tree that enforces a strict ordering rule on every node in the structure.
Elements.Notes: This property must hold recursively for every node in the tree, not just immediate parent-child pairs.

Items.Text: For any given node, all values in its left subtree must be less than the node's value.
For any given node, all values in its right subtree must be greater than the node's value.
This ordering property is what distinguishes a BST from a general binary tree.
Duplicate values are typically disallowed or handled by a consistent rule, such as placing them in the right subtree.

Elements.Title: BST Node Structure
Elements.BodyText: Each node in a Binary Search Tree holds a value and references to its left and right children, forming the backbone of the tree.
Elements.Notes: In JavaScript, a BST node can be represented as an object with a 'value' property and 'left' and 'right' pointer properties initialized to null.

Items.Text: A node stores a data value (also called a key) used for comparisons during search and insertion.
The left pointer references the root of the left subtree, containing smaller values.
The right pointer references the root of the right subtree, containing greater values.
Leaf nodes have both left and right pointers set to null, indicating no children.

Elements.Title: How BST Ordering Enables Efficient Search
Elements.BodyText: The ordering property of a BST allows search operations to eliminate half of the remaining nodes at each step, similar to a binary search on a sorted array.
Elements.Notes: This divide-and-conquer behavior is what gives BSTs their efficiency advantage over unsorted data structures like linked lists.

Items.Text: When searching for a value, compare it to the current node's value to decide whether to go left or right.
If the target is less than the current node, the entire right subtree can be ignored, and the search continues left.
If the target is greater than the current node, the entire left subtree is discarded and the search continues right.
In a balanced BST, this process results in O(log n) average time complexity for search operations.

Elements.Title: BST vs. General Binary Tree
Elements.BodyText: While all BSTs are binary trees, not all binary trees are BSTs — the key distinction lies in the presence of the ordering constraint.
Elements.Notes: A binary tree with nodes arranged randomly may require visiting every node to find a value, whereas a BST can guide the search directionally.

Items.Text: A general binary tree places no restrictions on how values are arranged relative to parent or sibling nodes.
A BST guarantees predictable value placement, making searches, insertions, and deletions more efficient.
Verifying whether a binary tree is a valid BST requires checking the ordering property across the entire tree, not just adjacent nodes.

Elements.Title: BST Height and Performance
Elements.BodyText: The efficiency of BST operations is directly tied to the height of the tree, which depends on the order in which values are inserted.
Elements.Notes: Inserting values in sorted order produces a degenerate (skewed) tree that behaves like a linked list with O(n) search time, highlighting the importance of balanced trees.

Items.Text: A balanced BST has roughly equal numbers of nodes on the left and right of each node, yielding O(log n) operation time.
An unbalanced or skewed BST can degrade to O(n) time for search, insertion, and deletion in the worst case.
The shape of the BST is determined by the sequence of inserted values, making insertion order a critical factor in performance.

Topics.Title: Insertion and Search Operations
Topics.Summary: Describes the logic for inserting new nodes into a BST while maintaining the ordering property, and outlines how search leverages that structure to locate values efficiently. Includes analysis of best and worst case performance.

Elements.Title: BST Ordering Property
Elements.BodyText: Every insertion and search operation in a BST relies on the fundamental ordering property that governs node placement.
Elements.Notes: This property is what distinguishes a BST from a general binary tree and is the foundation of its efficiency.

Items.Text: For any given node, all values in its left subtree must be less than the node's value.
All values in the right subtree must be greater than the node's value.
This property must hold recursively for every node in the tree, not just the root.
Duplicate values are typically handled by convention — either disallowed or consistently placed to the left or right.

Elements.Title: Insertion Logic
Elements.BodyText: Inserting a new node into a BST follows a recursive or iterative comparison process that preserves the ordering property.
Elements.Notes: Example: Inserting 7 into a tree rooted at 10 — compare 7 < 10, move left; if the left child is null, place 7 there.

Items.Text: Begin at the root and compare the new value to the current node's value.
If the new value is less, move to the left child; if greater, move to the right child.
Repeat the comparison at each subsequent node until a null position is reached.
Insert the new node at that null position, maintaining the BST ordering property.

Elements.Title: JavaScript Implementation of Insertion
Elements.BodyText: In JavaScript, BST insertion can be implemented as a recursive method on the tree class that navigates to the correct position.
Elements.Notes: A typical implementation defines a Node class with value, left, and right properties, and an insert method on the BST class that calls a recursive helper.

Items.Text: A Node object is created with the new value and null left and right pointers.
If the tree is empty, the new node becomes the root.
A recursive helper function compares the value at each node and traverses left or right until a null child slot is found.
The new node is assigned to that null child slot, completing the insertion.

Elements.Title: Search Logic
Elements.BodyText: Searching a BST exploits the ordering property to eliminate half of the remaining nodes at each comparison step.
Elements.Notes: This divide-and-conquer behavior is analogous to binary search on a sorted array, making BST search highly efficient in balanced trees.

Items.Text: Start at the root and compare the target value to the current node's value.
If the target equals the current node's value, the search is successful and returns that node.
If the target is less, recurse or iterate into the left subtree; if greater, move into the right subtree.
If a null node is reached without finding the target, the value does not exist in the tree.

Elements.Title: Best Case Performance
Elements.BodyText: In the best case, both insertion and search operate in O(log n) time when the BST is balanced.
Elements.Notes: A perfectly balanced BST with n nodes has a height of log₂(n), meaning at most log₂(n) comparisons are needed to reach any node.

Items.Text: A balanced tree distributes nodes evenly across left and right subtrees at every level.
Each comparison step halves the number of remaining nodes to consider, yielding logarithmic time complexity.
O(log n) performance makes BSTs competitive with other efficient data structures for search-heavy workloads.

Elements.Title: Worst Case Performance
Elements.BodyText: In the worst case, a BST degrades to O(n) time for insertion and search when the tree becomes skewed or unbalanced.
Elements.Notes: Inserting values in sorted order (e.g., 1, 2, 3, 4, 5) produces a tree that resembles a linked list, with all nodes on one side.

Items.Text: A skewed tree forms when nodes are inserted in ascending or descending sorted order.
Each new node is placed as the rightmost or leftmost child, creating a linear chain with height n.
Search and insertion must traverse all n nodes in the worst case, resulting in O(n) time complexity.
Self-balancing trees such as AVL trees or Red-Black trees are used to prevent this worst-case degradation.

Elements.Title: Comparing Best and Worst Case Scenarios
Elements.BodyText: Understanding the gap between best and worst case performance helps developers make informed decisions about when and how to use BSTs.
Elements.Notes: The average case for a randomly constructed BST is O(log n), but this cannot be guaranteed without balancing mechanisms.

Items.Text: Best case O(log n) occurs with a balanced tree; worst case O(n) occurs with a fully skewed tree.
The shape of the tree — determined by insertion order — is the primary factor influencing performance.
Randomly ordered input data tends to produce reasonably balanced trees in practice, approximating O(log n) on average.
For production systems requiring guaranteed performance, self-balancing BST variants should be preferred over plain BSTs.

Topics.Title: Deletion Operations
Topics.Summary: Covers the three cases of node deletion in a BST: removing a leaf, a node with one child, and a node with two children. Explains how the in-order successor or predecessor is used to preserve BST integrity.

Elements.Title: Overview of BST Deletion Cases
Elements.BodyText: Deleting a node from a Binary Search Tree requires handling three distinct cases depending on the structure of the node being removed.
Elements.Notes: Failing to handle all three cases correctly can corrupt the BST property, where all left descendants are less than the node and all right descendants are greater.

Items.Text: The three cases are: the target node is a leaf, the target node has one child, or the target node has two children.
Each case requires a different strategy to maintain the BST ordering property after removal.
Identifying which case applies is the first step before performing any pointer updates.

Elements.Title: Case 1: Deleting a Leaf Node
Elements.BodyText: A leaf node has no children, making it the simplest case to handle during deletion.
Elements.Notes: For example, deleting node 7 in a tree where 7 is a leaf simply requires setting its parent's left or right pointer to null.

Items.Text: Since the leaf has no children, it can be removed directly without affecting any other nodes.
The parent node's reference (left or right pointer) that pointed to the deleted leaf is set to null.
No restructuring of the tree is required after the removal.

Elements.Title: Case 2: Deleting a Node with One Child
Elements.BodyText: When the node to be deleted has exactly one child, that child takes the place of the deleted node in the tree.
Elements.Notes: For example, if node 15 has only a right child node 20, deleting 15 means the parent of 15 now points directly to 20.

Items.Text: The deleted node is bypassed by linking its parent directly to its single child.
The BST property is preserved because the subtree rooted at the child was already correctly ordered relative to the parent.
It does not matter whether the single child is a left or right child; the bypass approach applies in both situations.

Elements.Title: Case 3: Deleting a Node with Two Children
Elements.BodyText: Deleting a node with two children is the most complex case, requiring a replacement value to maintain BST integrity.
Elements.Notes: This is the most commonly tested case in technical interviews and requires careful pointer manipulation to avoid losing subtrees.

Items.Text: The deleted node's value is replaced by either its in-order successor or its in-order predecessor.
After copying the replacement value into the target node, the replacement node itself is then deleted from its original position.
Because the replacement node (in-order successor or predecessor) has at most one child, its own deletion falls into Case 1 or Case 2.

Elements.Title: The In-Order Successor
Elements.BodyText: The in-order successor of a node is the smallest node in its right subtree, and it is the most commonly used replacement when deleting a node with two children.
Elements.Notes: In JavaScript, finding the in-order successor involves traversing right once, then following left pointers until reaching a node with no left child.

Items.Text: The in-order successor is guaranteed to be greater than all nodes in the left subtree of the deleted node, preserving BST order.
It is also less than all remaining nodes in the right subtree, satisfying the BST property on the right side as well.
The in-order successor will have at most one child (a right child), simplifying its own removal.

Elements.Title: The In-Order Predecessor
Elements.BodyText: The in-order predecessor is the largest node in the left subtree and serves as an alternative replacement node during deletion.
Elements.Notes: Either the in-order successor or predecessor produces a valid BST after deletion; the choice is typically a matter of implementation preference or balancing strategy.

Items.Text: The in-order predecessor is found by traversing left once from the target node, then following right pointers to the rightmost node.
It is smaller than all nodes in the right subtree and larger than all remaining nodes in the left subtree.
Like the in-order successor, the predecessor has at most one child, making its subsequent deletion straightforward.

Elements.Title: Preserving BST Integrity After Deletion
Elements.BodyText: Regardless of which case applies, the fundamental goal of deletion is to ensure the BST property holds for every node in the tree after the operation.
Elements.Notes: In JavaScript implementations, deletion is often written as a recursive function that returns the updated subtree root after each recursive call, naturally handling pointer updates.

Items.Text: After any deletion, every node's left subtree must contain only values less than that node, and the right subtree must contain only greater values.
Using the in-order successor or predecessor as a replacement guarantees this property is maintained without requiring a full tree reorganization.
Testing deletion with nodes at various positions (root, internal nodes, leaves) is essential to verify the BST remains valid.

Topics.Title: Tree Traversal Strategies
Topics.Summary: Explores in-order, pre-order, and post-order traversal algorithms, detailing the sequence in which nodes are visited for each approach. Highlights practical use cases such as sorted output and tree serialization.

Elements.Title: In-Order Traversal (Left → Root → Right)
Elements.BodyText: In-order traversal visits the left subtree first, then the root node, and finally the right subtree, producing nodes in ascending sorted order for a binary search tree.
Elements.Notes: Example: For a BST with root 5, left child 3, and right child 7, in-order traversal yields 3 → 5 → 7. This property makes it ideal for extracting sorted data from a BST.

Items.Text: The algorithm recursively traverses the left subtree before processing the current node.
After visiting the root, the right subtree is traversed recursively in the same manner.
In a valid BST, in-order traversal always produces values in non-decreasing order.
Common use cases include sorting data stored in a BST and validating that a tree satisfies BST properties.

Elements.Title: Pre-Order Traversal (Root → Left → Right)
Elements.BodyText: Pre-order traversal visits the root node first, then recursively traverses the left subtree, followed by the right subtree.
Elements.Notes: Pre-order traversal is especially useful for serializing or copying a tree, because the root is always encountered before its children, preserving the structural hierarchy.

Items.Text: Processing the root before its children means the traversal captures the tree's top-down structure.
Pre-order output can be used to reconstruct the exact same tree if reinserted in the same sequence.
This traversal is commonly applied in tree serialization, expression tree evaluation, and directory structure printing.
Pre-order traversal forms the basis for depth-first search strategies in tree-based algorithms.

Elements.Title: Post-Order Traversal (Left → Right → Root)
Elements.BodyText: Post-order traversal recursively visits the left subtree and right subtree before processing the root node, ensuring children are always handled before their parent.
Elements.Notes: A classic example is evaluating arithmetic expression trees, where operands (children) must be resolved before applying the operator (parent node).

Items.Text: Both subtrees are fully visited before the current node is processed, making it suitable for bottom-up operations.
Post-order traversal is used when deleting a tree, since child nodes must be removed before their parent to avoid memory leaks.
It is also applied in computing the size or height of a tree, as child results are needed before calculating the parent's value.
Expression trees representing mathematical formulas use post-order traversal to produce postfix (Reverse Polish) notation.

Elements.Title: Recursive vs. Iterative Traversal Implementations
Elements.BodyText: All three traversal strategies can be implemented either recursively using the call stack or iteratively using an explicit stack data structure.
Elements.Notes: In JavaScript, recursive implementations are concise and readable but may cause stack overflow on very deep trees. Iterative implementations avoid this risk at the cost of slightly more complex code.

Items.Text: Recursive implementations naturally mirror the definition of each traversal, calling the function on left and right children as needed.
Iterative implementations use a stack to simulate the call stack, manually tracking which nodes still need to be visited.
For in-order iterative traversal, nodes are pushed onto the stack as the algorithm moves left, then popped and processed before moving right.
Choosing between recursive and iterative approaches depends on tree depth, language stack limits, and code readability requirements.

Elements.Title: Practical Use Cases for Each Traversal
Elements.BodyText: Each traversal strategy is suited to specific real-world tasks, and selecting the correct one is critical to solving tree-based problems efficiently.
Elements.Notes: Understanding when to apply each traversal strategy reduces time complexity and leads to more elegant, maintainable solutions in software development.

Items.Text: In-order traversal is used whenever sorted output from a BST is required, such as implementing sorted lists or range queries.
Pre-order traversal is preferred for tree serialization, cloning, and scenarios where parent context must be established before processing children.
Post-order traversal is applied in scenarios requiring aggregation from leaves upward, such as computing subtree sizes, heights, or deleting nodes safely.
Recognizing the traversal pattern needed is a key problem-solving skill in technical interviews and algorithm design.

Elements.Title: Traversal and Tree Serialization
Elements.BodyText: Tree serialization is the process of converting a tree structure into a linear sequence of values that can be stored or transmitted and later reconstructed.
Elements.Notes: Pre-order traversal combined with null markers (e.g., representing absent children as 'null') is a common serialization format, enabling full tree reconstruction from the sequence alone.

Items.Text: Serialization requires capturing enough structural information so the original tree can be rebuilt exactly from the output sequence.
Pre-order traversal naturally encodes parent-before-child relationships, making deserialization straightforward using a queue or index pointer.
In-order traversal alone is insufficient for serialization of general binary trees, as multiple tree shapes can produce the same in-order sequence.
Combining pre-order with in-order sequences uniquely identifies a binary tree and is a classic technique for full tree reconstruction.

Topics.Title: Implementing a BST in JavaScript
Topics.Summary: Guides students through a hands-on JavaScript implementation of a binary search tree, including class and method definitions for insertion, deletion, and traversal. Reinforces conceptual understanding through working code examples.

Elements.Title: Defining the Node Class
Elements.BodyText: Every BST is built from individual nodes, each storing a value and references to its left and right children. In JavaScript, a Node class provides the blueprint for these building blocks.
Elements.Notes: Example: class Node { constructor(value) { this.value = value; this.left = null; this.right = null; } }

Items.Text: Each node holds a single data value and two child pointers, initialized to null when the node is first created.
Using a class makes it easy to instantiate new nodes consistently throughout insertion and other operations.
Setting left and right to null by default correctly represents a leaf node with no children.

Elements.Title: Setting Up the BinarySearchTree Class
Elements.BodyText: The BST itself is represented as a class that tracks the root node and exposes methods for insertion, deletion, and traversal.
Elements.Notes: Example: class BinarySearchTree { constructor() { this.root = null; } }

Items.Text: The constructor initializes the root to null, representing an empty tree before any values are inserted.
All BST operations — insert, delete, search, and traversal — are defined as methods on this class, keeping logic organized and reusable.
Encapsulating the root reference inside the class prevents external code from accidentally corrupting the tree structure.

Elements.Title: Implementing the Insert Method
Elements.BodyText: The insert method adds a new value to the correct position in the BST by comparing the new value against existing nodes and traversing left or right accordingly.
Elements.Notes: A recursive helper function is a clean approach: if the current node is null, place the new node there; otherwise, compare values and recurse into the left or right subtree.

Items.Text: If the tree is empty, the new node becomes the root directly.
At each node, if the new value is less than the current node's value, insertion continues into the left subtree; if greater, into the right subtree.
Duplicate values are typically ignored or handled by a defined convention, such as placing them to the right.
Recursion naturally mirrors the tree's branching structure and simplifies the insertion logic.

Elements.Title: Implementing Search / Lookup
Elements.BodyText: A search method traverses the BST to determine whether a given value exists, exploiting the ordered property to eliminate half the remaining tree at each step.
Elements.Notes: The method returns true or the found node if the value exists, and false or null otherwise, depending on the intended use.

Items.Text: Starting at the root, compare the target value to the current node's value to decide whether to go left, right, or return a match.
If a null node is reached without finding the value, the value is not present in the tree.
Search runs in O(log n) time for a balanced BST, degrading to O(n) in the worst case of a skewed tree.

Elements.Title: Implementing BST Traversal Methods
Elements.BodyText: Traversal methods — in-order, pre-order, and post-order — are implemented as recursive functions that visit nodes in a specific sequence and collect or process their values.
Elements.Notes: In-order traversal (left → root → right) produces a sorted array of values from a BST, which is a useful built-in property to highlight with a working code example.

Items.Text: In-order traversal visits the left subtree, then the current node, then the right subtree, yielding values in ascending sorted order.
Pre-order traversal visits the current node first, making it useful for serializing or copying the tree structure.
Post-order traversal visits both subtrees before the current node, which is well-suited for deletion or memory cleanup operations.
Each traversal can be implemented by passing a callback or pushing values into an array that is returned at the end of the recursion.

Elements.Title: Implementing the Delete Method
Elements.BodyText: Deletion is the most complex BST operation because removing a node must preserve the BST ordering property, with different logic required depending on whether the node has zero, one, or two children.
Elements.Notes: For a node with two children, find the in-order successor (smallest value in the right subtree), copy its value to the current node, then delete the successor from the right subtree.

Items.Text: Deleting a leaf node (no children) is straightforward: set the parent's corresponding pointer to null.
Deleting a node with one child replaces the node with its only child, maintaining the tree's connectivity.
Deleting a node with two children requires finding the in-order successor or predecessor to substitute the deleted node's value before removing the successor.
The delete method is typically implemented recursively, returning the updated subtree root at each level so parent pointers are automatically updated.

Elements.Title: Putting It All Together: Testing the BST
Elements.BodyText: After implementing the Node and BinarySearchTree classes with insert, delete, search, and traversal methods, testing with concrete examples validates the implementation and reinforces understanding.
Elements.Notes: A simple test sequence: instantiate the BST, insert several values, call in-order traversal to confirm sorted output, search for existing and missing values, delete a node, and traverse again to verify correctness.

Items.Text: Inserting a known set of values and running in-order traversal should always produce those values in ascending sorted order.
Testing deletion for all three cases — leaf, one child, two children — ensures the method handles every scenario correctly.
Console logging or returning arrays from traversal methods makes it easy to visually verify the tree's state at each step.
Edge cases such as deleting the root node, searching an empty tree, or inserting duplicates should be explicitly tested.
